records.ety: [Through French, fr. L. conceptus a conceiving, conception, fr. concipere to conceive: cf. OF. p. p. nom. conciez conceived. See Conceive, and cf. Concept, Deceit.]

defs: That which is conceived, imagined, or formed in the mind; idea; thought; image; conception.
quotes: In laughing, there ever procedeth a conceit of somewhat ridiculous.
A man wise in his own conceit.

defs: Faculty of conceiving ideas; mental faculty; apprehension; as, a man of quick conceit.
quotes: How often, alas! did her eyes say unto me that they loved! and yet I, not looking for such a matter, had not my conceit open to understand them.

defs: Quickness of apprehension; active imagination; lively fancy.
quotes: His wit's as thick as Tewksbury mustard; there's more conceit in him than is in a mallet.

defs: A fanciful, odd, or extravagant notion; a quaint fancy; an unnatural or affected conception; a witty thought or turn of expression; a fanciful device; a whim; a quip.
quotes: On his way to the gibbet, a freak took him in the head to go off with a conceit.
Some to conceit alone their works confine, And glittering thoughts struck out at every line.
Tasso is full of conceits . . . which are not only below the dignity of heroic verse but contrary to its nature.

defs: An overweening idea of one's self; vanity.
quotes: Plumed with conceit he calls aloud.
The strong, by conceiting themselves weak, are therebly rendered as inactive . . . as if they really were so.
One of two bad ways you must conceit me, Either a coward or a flatterer.

defs: To form an idea; to think.
quotes: Those whose . . . vulgar apprehensions conceit but low of matrimonial purposes.
